title: Exercises: Molecular Properties from HF
description: Practice calculating molecular properties from HF results

exercises.title: Dipole Moment Calculation
exercises.description: Calculate molecular dipole moment from density and integrals.
exercises.instructions: Implement a function to calculate the electric dipole moment from the density matrix, dipole integrals, and nuclear coordinates.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def calculate_dipole(P, dipole_ints, nuclear_charges, nuclear_coords):
    """Calculate molecular dipole moment.
    
    Args:
        P: Density matrix (n_ao × n_ao)
        dipole_ints: Dipole integrals (3 × n_ao × n_ao) for x, y, z
        nuclear_charges: Array of nuclear charges
        nuclear_coords: Array of nuclear coordinates (n_atoms × 3)
    
    Returns:
        dipole: 3-component dipole moment in atomic units
    """
    # Your code here
    # μ = Σₐ Zₐ Rₐ - Tr(P @ D)
    pass

def au_to_debye(dipole_au):
    """Convert atomic units to Debye."""
    # 1 a.u. = 2.5417 Debye
    pass

# Test with H2O-like data
P = np.eye(5) * 2  # Simplified density
dipole_ints = np.zeros((3, 5, 5))
dipole_ints[2, 0, 0] = 0.5  # z-component

charges = np.array([8.0, 1.0, 1.0])  # O, H, H
coords = np.array([[0.0, 0.0, 0.117],
                   [0.0, 0.757, -0.469],
                   [0.0, -0.757, -0.469]])  # in Angstrom

mu = calculate_dipole(P, dipole_ints, charges, coords)
mu_D = au_to_debye(mu)
print(f"Dipole moment: {np.linalg.norm(mu_D):.2f} Debye")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def calculate_dipole(P, dipole_ints, nuclear_charges, nuclear_coords):
    """Calculate molecular dipole moment."""
    # Nuclear contribution
    mu_nuclear = np.zeros(3)
    for Z, R in zip(nuclear_charges, nuclear_coords):
        mu_nuclear += Z * R
    
    # Electronic contribution (negative of trace)
    mu_electronic = np.zeros(3)
    for i in range(3):
        mu_electronic[i] = -np.trace(P @ dipole_ints[i])
    
    return mu_nuclear + mu_electronic

def au_to_debye(dipole_au):
    """Convert atomic units to Debye."""
    return dipole_au * 2.5417

# Test
P = np.eye(5) * 2
dipole_ints = np.zeros((3, 5, 5))
dipole_ints[2, 0, 0] = 0.5

charges = np.array([8.0, 1.0, 1.0])
coords = np.array([[0.0, 0.0, 0.117],
                   [0.0, 0.757, -0.469],
                   [0.0, -0.757, -0.469]])

mu = calculate_dipole(P, dipole_ints, charges, coords)
mu_D = au_to_debye(mu)
print(f"Dipole moment: {np.linalg.norm(mu_D):.2f} Debye")
hints: Nuclear dipole: sum of Z × R over all atoms
Electronic dipole: -Tr(P @ D) for each component

exercises.title: Mulliken Population Analysis
exercises.instructions: Write functions to compute Mulliken populations and charges from density and overlap matrices.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def mulliken_populations(P, S, ao_to_atom):
    """Calculate Mulliken populations per atom.
    
    Args:
        P: Density matrix
        S: Overlap matrix
        ao_to_atom: List mapping AO index to atom index
    
    Returns:
        populations: Electron population per atom
    """

    # Total electrons = Tr(PS)
    # Population on A = Σμ∈A (PS)μμ
    pass

def mulliken_charges(populations, nuclear_charges):
    """Calculate Mulliken charges from populations."""
    # Q_A = Z_A - population_A
    pass

# Test: H2 molecule
P = np.array([[1.0, 0.5],
              [0.5, 1.0]])
S = np.array([[1.0, 0.6],
              [0.6, 1.0]])
ao_to_atom = [0, 1]  # AO 0 on atom 0, AO 1 on atom 1
nuclear_charges = [1, 1]  # Two hydrogens

pops = mulliken_populations(P, S, ao_to_atom)
charges = mulliken_charges(pops, nuclear_charges)

print(f"Populations: {pops}")
print(f"Charges: {charges}")
print(f"Total electrons: {sum(pops):.2f}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def mulliken_populations(P, S, ao_to_atom):
    """Calculate Mulliken populations per atom."""
    PS = P @ S
    n_atoms = max(ao_to_atom) + 1
    populations = np.zeros(n_atoms)
    
    for mu, atom in enumerate(ao_to_atom):
        populations[atom] += PS[mu, mu]
    
    return populations

def mulliken_charges(populations, nuclear_charges):
    """Calculate Mulliken charges from populations."""
    charges = np.array(nuclear_charges) - populations
    return charges

# Test
P = np.array([[1.0, 0.5], [0.5, 1.0]])
S = np.array([[1.0, 0.6], [0.6, 1.0]])
ao_to_atom = [0, 1]
nuclear_charges = [1, 1]

pops = mulliken_populations(P, S, ao_to_atom)
charges = mulliken_charges(pops, nuclear_charges)

print(f"Populations: {pops}")
print(f"Charges: {charges}")
print(f"Total electrons: {sum(pops):.2f}")
hints: Sum diagonal elements for each atom

exercises.title: Frequency Scale Factors
exercises.description: Apply scale factors to HF vibrational frequencies.
exercises.instructions:
Given HF/6-31G* calculated frequencies for H₂O:
- ν₁ = 4189 cm⁻¹ (symmetric stretch)
- ν₂ = 1827 cm⁻¹ (bend)
- ν₃ = 4302 cm⁻¹ (asymmetric stretch)

a) Apply the HF/6-31G* scale factor of 0.8929
b) Compare with experimental values: 3657, 1595, 3756 cm⁻¹
c) Calculate the percent error for each mode

solution.answer: a) 3740, 1631, 3841 cm⁻¹; b) All ~2-3% high; c) Average error 2.3%

hints: Simply multiply each frequency by the scale factor
Scaled HF frequencies are quite accurate

exercises.title: One-Electron Property Matrix
exercises.description: Calculate an expectation value from property integrals.
exercises.instructions: Write a general function to compute one-electron property expectation values.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def expectation_value(P, O):
    """Calculate one-electron expectation value.
    
    ⟨Ô⟩ = Tr(P @ O) = Σμν Pμν Oνμ
    
    Args:
        P: Density matrix
        O: Property operator matrix in AO basis
    
    Returns:
        Expectation value
    """
    # Your code here
    pass

# Test: kinetic energy
P = np.array([[1.5, 0.3],
              [0.3, 0.5]])
T = np.array([[1.0, 0.2],
              [0.2, 0.8]])  # Kinetic energy integrals

T_exp = expectation_value(P, T)
print(f"Kinetic energy: {T_exp:.4f} Hartree")

# Test: overlap (should give number of electrons)
S = np.array([[1.0, 0.3],
              [0.3, 1.0]])
N = expectation_value(P, S)
print(f"Number of electrons: {N:.2f}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def expectation_value(P, O):
    """Calculate one-electron expectation value."""
    return np.trace(P @ O)

# Test
P = np.array([[1.5, 0.3], [0.3, 0.5]])
T = np.array([[1.0, 0.2], [0.2, 0.8]])

T_exp = expectation_value(P, T)
print(f"Kinetic energy: {T_exp:.4f} Hartree")

S = np.array([[1.0, 0.3], [0.3, 1.0]])
N = expectation_value(P, S)
print(f"Number of electrons: {N:.2f}")
hints: With overlap matrix, this gives electron count

exercises.title: Hellmann-Feynman Theorem
exercises.description: Derive forces from the Hellmann-Feynman theorem.
exercises.instructions: Show that for exact wavefunctions, the force on nucleus A is: F_A = -⟨Ψ|∂Ĥ/∂R_A|Ψ⟩. Explain why HF needs 'Pulay corrections'.

proof: For an exact eigenstate Ψ of Ĥ with E = ⟨Ψ|Ĥ|Ψ⟩:
In HF with a finite basis set, the wavefunction is NOT an exact eigenstate.
The basis functions depend on nuclear positions (atom-centered).
When we move nuclei, basis functions move too!
Pulay correction accounts for these extra terms.
solution.answer: The theorem gives F = -⟨∂Ĥ/∂R⟩ for exact states. HF uses atom-centered basis functions that move with nuclei, creating extra derivative terms (Pulay forces) beyond Hellmann-Feynman.

hints: For HF, basis functions are not fixed in space
